The list reads like a royal banquet menu rather than a simple daily tally — the point is abundance and variety as markers of Solomon's prosperity and the empire's resources.
4.24:
v24 — Dominion west of the Euphrates; peace on all sides
'Ki hu rodeh be-khol-ever ha-nahar' — 'For he had dominion over all the region west (ever) of the Euphrates.' The word ever (side/region) gives Aramaic-speakers the name for the western province: 'Abar Nahara' (Across the River) — later the Persian satrapy name. 'Ve-shalom hayah lo mi-kol-evadav savio' — 'And he had peace from all his neighbors on all sides.' Peace as the mark of covenant blessing (Lev 26:6: 'I will give peace in the land') is Solomon's defining political achievement.
4.25:
v25 — Each man under his vine and fig tree, Dan to Beersheba
'Va-yeshev Yehudah ve-Yisrael lavetach…ish tachat gafno ve-tachat te'enato' — 'Judah and Israel lived in safety, each man under his vine and fig tree.' The phrase 'under his vine and fig tree' is the quintessential biblical image of security and prosperity (Mic 4:4; Zech 3:10; cf. Rev 22). Dan to Beersheba — the traditional northern and southern limits of Israelite territory — frames the peace as encompassing all of the land. This verse is the peak of the Solomonic narrative's idealized portrait.
4.26:
v26 — Forty thousand horse stalls and twelve thousand horsemen
Solomon had forty thousand stalls for chariot horses (susim le-markavto) and twelve thousand horsemen (parashim). Deuteronomy 17:16 explicitly prohibits the king from multiplying horses or returning to Egypt for horses. The scale of Solomon's cavalry establishment — the largest in the ancient Near East at this time — directly violates this command. The narrator records the fact without immediate comment, but the Deuteronomic law hangs over the description: Solomon's military power is built precisely on what the Torah forbade.
4.27:
v27 — District officers supply the palace without lack
The twelve district officers fulfill their provision obligation to King Solomon and all who come to his table, each in his month. Nothing is lacking (lo ye'adder davar). The efficiency of the provision system is its own commendation: the administrative machinery works. But the human cost — thirty thousand Israelites conscripted for labor (ch5), twelve districts taxed monthly — is the shadow behind the adequacy.
4.28:
v28 — Barley and straw for the horses
They also bring barley (se'orah) and straw (teven) for the horses and swift steeds (ha-rekesh) to the designated places. The feeding of the royal cavalry requires a separate supply chain beyond the human food provision. The agricultural output of twelve districts feeds both the palace and its military infrastructure. The detail is prosaic but significant: empire runs on logistics, and Solomon's empire has mastered that logistics at scale.
4.30:
v30 — Solomon's wisdom exceeded all the sages of the east and Egypt
'Va-tereb chokhmah Shlomo me-chokhmah kol-benei qedem u-mikol chokhmah Mitzrayim' — 'Solomon's wisdom exceeded the wisdom of all the sons of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.' The benei qedem (sons of the east) refers to the wisdom traditions of Mesopotamia, Arabia, and the Transjordanian regions — the famous wise men of the ancient world. Egypt's wisdom tradition was the oldest and most developed in the ancient Near East. Surpassing both is a way of claiming the highest conceivable wisdom for Solomon.
4.31:
v31 — Wiser than Ethan, Heman, Chalcol, and Darda
Named comparators: Ethan the Ezrahite (possibly the author of Psalm 89), Heman (possibly the author of Psalm 88), Chalcol, and Darda — sons of Mahol. These figures are legendary wise men of Israelite tradition; some appear in 1 Chronicles 2:6 as descendants of Judah through Zerah. The comparison presupposes a known wisdom tradition with recognized exemplars. Solomon surpasses them all. Va-yehi shmo be-khol-ha-goyim saviv — 'His fame was in all the surrounding nations.' Wisdom generates international reputation.
4.32:
v32 — Three thousand proverbs; one thousand and five songs
Solomon composed three thousand meshalim (proverbs/sayings/comparisons) and one thousand and five shirim (songs). The numbers are traditionally large — a way of expressing inexhaustible literary output. The book of Proverbs contains far fewer than three thousand sayings attributed to Solomon; the tradition assumes a larger Solomonic corpus, most of which has not been preserved. Mashal covers not just proverbs but parables, allegories, and comparative wisdom forms — the breadth of the genre matches the breadth of Solomon's knowledge.
4.33:
v33 — Botanical and zoological knowledge from cedar to hyssop
Solomon spoke about plant life (vayedaber al-ha-etzim) from the cedar of Lebanon (erez ha-Levanon — the prestige tree of the ancient world) to the hyssop (ezov) growing from the wall — the smallest, most humble plant. He spoke about animals (behemah), birds (of), reptiles (remes), and fish (dagah). This is encyclopedic natural knowledge, the ancient equivalent of natural philosophy. The range from cedar to hyssop (large to small) and through four animal categories represents comprehensive creation-knowledge — the wisdom of the creature-world as a whole.
4.34:
v34 — Nations send to hear Solomon's wisdom
'Va-yavo'u mi-kol-ha-ammim lishmo'a et chokhmah Shlomo' — 'They came from all peoples to hear the wisdom of Solomon, sent by all the kings of the earth who had heard of his wisdom.' The wisdom draws international pilgrimage: Solomon becomes a destination, not just a power. The Queen of Sheba's visit (ch10) is the narrative expansion of this summary. The phrase kol-malkhei ha-aretz (all the kings of the earth) is hyperbolic but intended to capture the universal scope of Solomon's reputation. Wisdom at its peak draws the world toward Israel.

5.4: Solomon’s explanation for why David could not build the temple — that YHWH has now given rest (mᵉnûḥāh) on every side — invokes the Deuteronomic theology of mᵉnûḥāh as the precondition for the permanent sanctuary. Deut 12:9-11 makes centralized worship conditional on YHWH giving Israel rest (hammᵉnûḥāh wᵉhannāḥălāh, ‘the rest and the inheritance’): ‘when he gives you rest from all your enemies around you... then to the place that YHWH your God will choose, to make his name dwell there, there you shall bring all that I command you.’ Mᵉnûḥāh is from the root nûaḥ (to rest, to settle), the same root as Noah’s name (Gen 5:29: ‘this one will give us rest’), the Ark’s settling on Ararat (Gen 8:4), and the Sabbath rest (Gen 2:2: wayyānaḥ). The temple is built in the condition of Sabbath-peace. Heb 3:7–4:11 reads mᵉnûḥāh as the theological category fulfilled by Christ: ‘we who have believed enter that rest (katapausin)’ (Heb 4:3), and the argument is that the temple’s built-in-rest was only a type — since Ps 95:11 (‘they shall not enter my rest’) was spoken after the Sinai-rest and the Canaan-rest had already been achieved, a further mᵉnûḥāh remains. Heb 4:10: ‘whoever has entered God’s rest has also rested from his works as God did from his.’
5.1:
v1 — Hiram king of Tyre sends to Solomon
Hiram (Chiram) of Tyre hears that Solomon has been anointed king in place of David and sends his servants — the diplomatic mission that will launch the temple timber partnership. Hiram is identified as one who had ohev David (loved David) throughout his days — the word ohev (love) is the diplomatic term for a treaty-relationship or alliance. The Phoenician-Israelite alliance predates Solomon; Hiram had supplied David with cedar for his palace (2 Sam 5:11). This is a relationship Solomon inherits and will use to far greater effect.
5.2:
v2 — Solomon responds to Hiram's embassy
The narrative compresses the diplomatic exchange: Hiram sends, and Solomon sends back (va-yishlach Shlomo el-Chiram). Solomon's letter is quoted in vv3-6, a formal diplomatic communication explaining his purpose and his need. The genre is ancient royal correspondence — similar in structure and tone to letters found in the Amarna archive and other ANE diplomatic collections. Solomon presents himself as the appropriate continuation of David's relationship with Hiram.
5.3:
v3 — David could not build because of war
Solomon rehearses to Hiram why David did not build the temple: 'attah yadata et David avi ki lo yakhal livnot bayit le-shem YHWH Elohav mipnei ha-milchamah' — 'You know that David my father could not build a house for the name of the LORD his God because of the wars on every side.' The formal reason is war: God told David that because he had shed much blood (damim rabbim shafakht), he would not build the temple (1 Chr 22:8; 28:3). Solomon is explaining this to Hiram as part of establishing that Solomon, not David, is the appropriate temple-builder — and the time is now right.
5.5:
v5 — I intend to build a house for the name of the LORD
'Va-ani amad livnot bayit le-shem YHWH Elohai' — 'And I intend to build a house for the name of the LORD my God.' The phrase le-shem YHWH (for the name of the LORD) is the Deuteronomic formula for legitimate worship centralization — the temple is the place where God's name dwells (Deut 12:5, 11; 14:23). The formula le-shem (for the name) rather than 'for the presence' or 'for the glory' reflects a sophisticated theology: God's presence is mediated through his name, not located in the building itself. Solomon then cites the Davidic promise from 2 Sam 7: 'Your son whom I will set on your throne shall build the house for my name.'
5.6:
v6 — Give orders for cedars; I will pay your wages
The practical request: 'Tzaveh ve-yikhretu-li arzim min-ha-Levanon' — 'Give orders for the cutting of cedars from Lebanon for me.' Cedar (erez) was the prestige building material of the ancient Near East — Phoenicia's major export product. Solomon's servants will work alongside Hiram's, and Solomon will pay whatever wages Hiram sets. The acknowledgment that 'you know there is no one among us who knows how to cut timber like the Sidonians' (ki ein banu yode'a lichtov etzim ka-Tzidoniim) is diplomatic honesty that also justifies the partnership. Israel has no timber and no woodcutters skilled in this work.
5.7:
v7 — Hiram rejoices greatly; blesses the LORD
'Va-yishmach Chiram me'od va-yomer Barukh YHWH ha-yom' — 'Hiram rejoiced greatly and said: Blessed be the LORD today.' A Phoenician king blesses YHWH — this is significant. Hiram does not convert or confess YHWH as sole God, but he acknowledges the covenant between YHWH and David as the ground of this partnership: 'who has given David a wise son over this great people.' The blessing formula (barukh YHWH) is standard Hebrew liturgical language; Hiram's use of it may reflect diplomatic courtesy or genuine recognition, but the narrator records it without qualification.
5.8:
v8 — Hiram accepts: I will supply all you desire
Hiram's formal acceptance: he has received Solomon's message and will do all that Solomon desires regarding cedar (arez) and cypress (berosh) timber. The pairing of cedar and cypress appears throughout the temple-building narrative — cedar for structural beams and paneling, cypress for flooring and doors. The specific species (berosh = probably Cupressus sempervirens or possibly juniper) were complementary in their working properties and both unavailable in Israel's hill country.
5.9:
v9 — Rafts floated from Lebanon to the sea; Israel will pay
The logistics: Hiram's servants will cut logs from Lebanon, bring them down to the sea (ha-yam), and float them as rafts (dobarot — log-rafts lashed together) to whatever location Solomon designates, where they will be disassembled and Solomon's servants will take them inland. The maritime timber trade is well-documented in the ancient world — Phoenician coastal expertise in sea-transport was the complement to their forestry. Hiram's request in return is that Solomon provide provisions for his household (natan mzon le-veiti).
5.10:
v10 — Hiram supplied all the cedar and cypress Solomon desired
'Va-yiten Chiram el-Shlomo atzei arzim va-atzei broshim kol-cheftzoh' — 'Hiram supplied Solomon with cedar and cypress timber, all his desire.' The brief summary of the exchange highlights the completeness of Hiram's fulfillment: kol cheftzoh (all his desire). In narrative terms, this signals that the material foundation of the temple is secured. The logistical challenge of moving massive timber from Lebanese forests to Jerusalem's heights is accomplished through the Phoenician partnership — something Israel could not have done alone.
5.11:
v11 — Solomon gives Hiram wheat and oil annually
Solomon gives Hiram twenty thousand kor of wheat (chittim) for his household and twenty thousand kor of pure beaten olive oil (shemen katit). The shemen katit (pressed/beaten oil — the highest quality, cold-pressed from hand-beaten olives) is distinguished from ordinary lamp oil. The quantities — twenty thousand cors each of grain and oil annually — represent a substantial fraction of Israelite agricultural surplus going to Phoenicia. This is the Solomonic economy at work: timber north, grain and oil south, sustained as long as the temple project runs.
5.12:
v12 — The LORD gave Solomon wisdom; a treaty of peace between them
'Va-YHWH natan chokhmah li-Shlomo ka'asher diber lo' — 'The LORD gave Solomon wisdom as he had promised him.' The Gibeon dream-promise is now being fulfilled in the international arena: the wisdom given there is expressed here in successful diplomacy. 'Va-yihi shalom bein Chiram u-vein Shlomo va-yikhredu berit shneihem' — 'There was peace between Hiram and Solomon, and the two of them made a covenant (berit).' The formal treaty (berit) between Israel and Phoenicia is both commercial and diplomatic, a formal alliance that will be renewed and fatefully extended in Ahab's marriage to Jezebel (1 Kings 16).
5.13:
v13 — Solomon conscripts thirty thousand Israelites for forced labor
'Va-ya'al ha-melekh Shlomo mas mi-kol-Yisrael' — 'King Solomon raised (ya'al) forced labor (mas) from all Israel — thirty thousand men.' The word mas (corvée/forced labor) again — this time explicitly applied to Israelites. The same term used for Egyptian slave labor, the same institution from which the Exodus liberated Israel, is now deployed by Israel's own king. The thirty thousand figure is massive. The narrative does not comment editorially, but the resonance with Exodus is unmistakable and will be made explicit in ch 12 when the northern tribes demand relief from this burden.
5.14:
v14 — Monthly rotation: one month in Lebanon, two months home
The rotation system: ten thousand men per month to Lebanon, two months at home. Adoniram (Adoram) is in charge of the mas. The rotation (one-in-three months in Lebanon) means any individual Israelite spends one third of the year doing royal labor in foreign forests. The organizational logic is relatively humane — rest periods are built in — but the compulsory nature and scale (30,000 men) make it a heavy imposition. Adoniram will be stoned by the northern tribes at Rehoboam's accession (1 Kings 12:18) — his role as corvée administrator makes him the face of the grievance.
5.15:
v15 — Seventy thousand carriers and eighty thousand stonecutters
In addition to the thirty thousand Israelite woodcutters sent to Lebanon, Solomon has seventy thousand savel (burden-bearers/carriers) and eighty thousand chotzev (stonecutters) in the hill country. The numbers — 180,000 laborers in total — are enormous. Most scholars understand these as primarily drawn from the Canaanite population (explicitly stated in 1 Kings 9:20-22 — Canaanite remnants as permanent forced laborers, while Israelites serve as soldiers, officers, and commanders). The distinction matters for the ch12 narrative: it is Israelite corvée that sparks the revolt, not Canaanite.
5.16:
v16 — Three thousand three hundred supervisors
Three thousand three hundred sarim ha-nitzavim (officer-overseers) supervised the workers. The supervision-to-labor ratio (1 supervisor per ~55 workers) reflects serious organizational scale. The 2 Chronicles parallel (2 Chr 2:2) says 3,600 supervisors — a well-known discrepancy probably due to different counting methods or textual transmission. The supervisory class represents Solomon's middle management: below the district officers, above the laborers, essential to translating royal ambition into physical construction.
5.17:
v17 — Large, expensive stones for the foundation
At the king's command, they quarry large, expensive (yeqarot — precious/costly) stones to lay the foundation of the temple with avnei gazit (dressed/cut stone). The avnei gazit (ashlar masonry — stones cut square with a saw) contrasts with the fieldstone construction of ordinary buildings. The archaeological record confirms massive ashlar construction at Solomon's Megiddo, Hazor, and Gezer — the 'six-chambered gates' attributed to this period show precisely this kind of monumental stonework. The temple's foundation was built to last.
5.18:
v18 — Three teams of builders: Israelite, Phoenician, and Gebalite
Solomon's builders, Hiram's builders, and the men of Gebal (Gebalim) cut and prepared timber and stones for the building. Gebal (Byblos in Greek — modern Jbeil in Lebanon) was an ancient Phoenician city famous for craftsmen and stonecutters, and gives its name to the Greek biblos (book) through its role as a papyrus trading center. Three-nation collaboration — Israel for the project, Tyre for timber and maritime logistics, Byblos for skilled stonecutting — reflects the international scope of what Solomon is building. The temple is a monument that could only exist at the intersection of Solomonic wealth, Phoenician skill, and prophetic commission.

15.14: The regnal evaluation of Asa — wᵉlēḇ ʾāsāʾ hāyāh šālēm ʿim YHWH kol yāmāyw, ‘Asa’s heart was wholly true (šālēm) to YHWH all his days’ — is the Deuteronomistic historian’s highest royal verdict. The adjective šālēm (whole, complete, undivided, at peace) is the root of šālôm (peace, well-being) and of Solomon’s name (šᵉlōmōh): the lēḇ šālēm is the heart that has found its integration and rest in YHWH alone, undivided by rival loyalties. 2 Chr 16:9 extends the principle as a divine promise: ‘the eyes of YHWH run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to give strong support to those whose heart is šālēm toward him.’ The regnal evaluations of Kings and Chronicles use the šālēm standard as their binary — each king either maintains wholeness or fragments it. Asa himself fails this standard in 2 Chr 16:7-12, where he trusts in the king of Aram rather than YHWH in his final years, and his heart ceases to be šālēm. The NT appropriation of the whole-heart standard: Deut 6:5 (‘you shall love YHWH your God with all your heart, bᵉkol lᵉḇāḇᵉḵā’) is what Jesus identifies in Matt 22:37 as the first and greatest commandment — the lēḇ šālēm as the definition of the covenant’s core demand. The fact that no king fully sustained the lēḇ šālēm across an entire reign is the OT’s implied argument for why the Davidic promise requires a greater son.
15.1: Aḇîyām (H38) vs. the parallel form Aḇîyāh (Chronicles) — both names mean 'my father is YHWH,' but 1 Kings consistently uses the -yām ending, possibly a textual variant or scribal preference. The regnal formula situates him in the synchronistic chronology: eighteenth year of Jeroboam son of Nebat, maintaining the dual-kingdom reckoning throughout the divided-monarchy narrative.
15.2: šālōš šānîm mālaḵ bîrûšālāim — three years, the shortest reign listed for any Davidic king until Ahaziah. His mother (ʾimmô) is Maacah daughter of Abishalom — the same woman who later appears as the gᵉḇîrāh (queen mother) in v.13. The Abishalom/Absalom identification is debated; she may be a granddaughter of David's son Absalom.
15.3: wayyēleḵ bᵉḵol-ḥaṭṭōʾt ʾāḇîw — he walked in all the sins of his father. The verb hālaḵ bᵉ- (to walk in/after) is the standard Deuteronomistic evaluative formula for apostasy; the preposition bᵉ signals immersion in a pattern of behavior, not single acts. Lᵉḇāḇô (his heart) qualified by šālēm (whole, complete) appears in v.14 as the contrast: Abijam's heart was not šālēm ʿim YHWH as his father David's was.
15.4: nîr (H5216) — often rendered 'lamp,' but the context here is dynastic: YHWH gives David a nîr in Jerusalem, meaning an heir-maintained flame of royal continuity. The term recurs in 11:36 and 15:4 as a covenant pledge. Hāqîm ʾeṯ-bᵉnô ʾaḥărāyw — raising up his son after him: the language of 2 Sam 7:12 (the Davidic covenant), explicitly invoked here to explain why an unworthy king still holds the throne.
15.5: The parenthetical exception — raq bᵉḏaḇar ʾûrîyāh haḥittî, 'except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite' — is the Deuteronomistic historian's only qualifying note on David's otherwise perfect record. The phrasing 'the Hittite' distinguishes Uriah by ethnicity and marks the crime as involving a non-Israelite loyal warrior, compounding the moral weight. This exception clause protects the absolute Davidic standard while acknowledging historical reality.
15.6: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-rᵉḥaḇʿām ûḇên-yārāḇʿām — war existed between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout Abijam's entire life (kol-yᵉmê ḥayyāyw). This is a redactional resumption of the conflict note already made in 14:30. The northern–southern border conflict defines the background of the Abijam and early Asa periods.
15.7: The regnal closing formula: wᵉyeter diḇrê ʾaḇîyām wᵉḵol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh — the rest of Abijam's acts and all he did are in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. This archival reference appears stereotypically throughout the divided monarchy; it implies a source document beyond the biblical text, now lost. The formula signals narrative closure before the accession notice.
15.8: wayyiškaḇ ʾaḇîyām ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw — the sleep-with-ancestors idiom for natural death, as opposed to 'he died' (wayyāmot), which may imply violent death. Burial in the city of David (ʿîr dāwîd) marks him as within the legitimate Davidic line despite his failure; David's tomb serves as the royal burial standard for Judah.
15.9: bišnat ʿeśrîm lᵉyārāḇʿām — synchronistic dating: Asa begins in Jeroboam's twentieth year. The dual-kingdom dating system requires the historian to maintain two parallel chronologies, cross-referenced at each accession. Asa's reign will span 41 years and overlap the reigns of six northern kings.
15.10: ʾaḇ-ʾimmô māʿăḵāh bat-ʾăḇîšālôm — his 'father's mother' (grandmother) was Maacah daughter of Abishalom, the same woman named as Abijam's mother in v.2. This likely reflects the same person serving as gᵉḇîrāh through two reigns, a well-attested ANE practice where the queen mother position was inherited. The Hebrew ʾēm can mean biological mother or functionary in the queen-mother role.
15.11: wayyaʿaś ʾāsāʾ hayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉdāwîd ʾāḇîw — the positive Davidic standard: Asa did what was right in YHWH's eyes, like David his father. This is the highest evaluative grade in the Deuteronomistic system. Only Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah receive unqualified positive evaluations; Asa's is qualified in v.14 by the high-places caveat.
15.12: wayyaʿaḇer haqqᵉdēšîm min-hāʾāreṣ — he expelled the qᵉdēšîm (H6945, masculine plural from qādôš) from the land. The term denotes male devotees associated with illicit cultic practice; the exact nature of their service remains debated (sacred prostitution vs. other cultic roles), but their association with non-YHWH worship is clear from the surrounding context. Wayyāsar ʾeṯ-kol-haggillulîm — and he removed all the gillulîm (H1544, 'dung-idols,' a pejorative for divine images). The root may relate to gālal (dung/rolling) — a deliberate slur against idol cults.
15.13: Already covered (v14 entry exists); this note is for v13: gᵉḇîrāh (H1377) — queen mother. The term is a semi-official title: the highest-ranking woman in the royal court, not merely a biological relation. Maacah's removal (wayyᵉsirēhā min-gᵉḇîrāh) for making a mipleset (H4656, a repulsive/abominable image) for Asherah is significant: this is the only biblical instance of a queen mother being deposed. Wayyiḵrōṯ ʾeṯ-mipleset and burning it at the Kidron valley — Naḥal qiḏrôn was the ritual dump for Jerusalem's cultic refuse.
15.15: wayyāḇēʾ ʾeṯ-qāḏᵉšê ʾāḇîw wᵉʾeṯ-qāḏᵉšāyw — he brought (bôʾ in hiphil) the things his father had dedicated (qāḏᵉšê ʾāḇîw) and the things he himself dedicated (qāḏᵉšāyw) into the house of YHWH. Qāḏᵉšîm here (different from v.12) means 'dedicated/sacred things' — gold, silver, vessels. This marks Asa as a temple-restorationist, consistent with his overall positive evaluation.
15.16: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-ʾāsāʾ ûḇên-baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl kol-yᵉmêhem — war existed between Asa and Baasha king of Israel throughout their days. The resumptive formula echoes v.6 and 14:30. This border warfare defines the political backdrop for Asa's appeal to Ben-hadad in v.18–20.
15.17: wayyaʿal baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl ʿal-yᵉhûḏāh wayyiḇen ʾeṯ-hārāmāh — Baasha marched up against Judah and built (bānāh) Ramah. Ramah (5 miles north of Jerusalem) sat astride the main north–south route; fortifying it would have strangled Jerusalem's trade and communication. Lᵉḇiltî tēt lāṣēt wālāḇôʾ lᵉʾāsāʾ — so that no one could leave or enter for Asa — describes a blockade function, not just a military fortification.
15.18: wayyiqqaḥ ʾāsāʾ ʾeṯ-kol-haḵkesef wᵉhazzāhāḇ — Asa took all the silver and gold from the treasuries of the house of YHWH and the house of the king and sent them to Ben-hadad. The emptying of the temple treasury for a military alliance is a recurring pattern in the Judean monarchy (cf. 14:26); here it is strategic desperation. Ḇên-hăḏaḏ ben-ṭaḇrimmôn — Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon, the Aramean king of Damascus. This is the first appearance of Ben-hadad I in the narrative.
15.19: bᵉrît bênî ûḇêneḵā — 'a covenant between me and you,' invoking a prior treaty between fathers. The bᵉrît language is politically freighted: it implies loyalty obligations. Asa sends the silver as a gift (šōḥad, bribe/tribute) to break Ben-hadad's existing treaty with Baasha — he is buying a realignment of Aramean foreign policy.
15.20: wayyišmaʿ ben-hăḏaḏ ʾel-hammelek ʾāsāʾ — Ben-hadad complied. He sent his army commanders against the cities of Israel, striking ʿîyyôn, dān, ʾāḇēl bêṯ-maʿăḵāh, and all Kinnereth — the northern Galilee region. This Aramean intervention into northern Israel's border region forced Baasha to abandon the Ramah project.
15.21: wayyihy kišmōaʿ baʿšāʾ wayyeḥdal miḇnôṯ ʾeṯ-hārāmāh — when Baasha heard, he stopped building Ramah and returned to Tirzah. The verb ḥādal (H2308, to cease/stop) marks the military-political success of Asa's diplomatic strategy. Tirzah was the northern kingdom's capital from Jeroboam to Omri.
15.22: wayyašmiʿ hammelek ʾāsāʾ kol-yᵉhûḏāh ʾên nāqî — Asa summoned all Judah, no one exempt (ʾên nāqî, no one released/innocent of the levy). This is a labor conscription (mas ʿōḇēḏ) applied to the entire male population. They removed the stones and timber Baasha had used at Ramah and built with them Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah — recycling enemy fortification material for Judean frontier defense.
15.23: The closing formula for Asa: wᵉḵol-gᵉḇûrāṯô, all his might, plus the note that in his old age (bᵉʿēṯ ziḵnāṯô) he was diseased in his feet (ḥālāh ʾeṯ-raglāyw). The foot disease may be gout or arteriosclerosis; 2 Chr 16:12 adds that he sought physicians rather than YHWH, a theological note absent here in Kings.
15.24: wayyiškaḇ ʾāsāʾ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqqāḇēr ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw bᵉʿîr dāwîd ʾāḇîw — Asa rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David his father. The burial formula confirms dynastic continuity: even qualified kings receive the Davidic burial honor. His son Jehoshaphat reigned in his place — the succession formula (wayyimloḵ yᵉhôšāpāṭ bᵉnô taḥtāyw).
15.25: wᵉnāḏāḇ ben-yārāḇʿām mālaḵ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl bišnat šᵉtayim lᵉʾāsāʾ — Nadab son of Jeroboam became king of Israel in Asa's second year, reigning two years. The synchronistic dating resumes for the northern kingdom. Nadab's brief reign anticipates the pattern of short, violent tenures that characterize the northern monarchy after Jeroboam.
15.26: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyēleḵ bᵉḏereḵ ʾāḇîw ûḇᵉḥaṭṭāʾṯô — the negative Jeroboamic evaluation formula, applied here to Nadab. Walking in the way (dereḵ) of his father and in the sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt) by which Jeroboam caused Israel to sin — this phrase recurs verbatim for every unqualified northern king, a Deuteronomistic refrain anchoring the entire narrative of northern failure to the founding apostasy.
15.27: wayyiqšor ʿālāyw baʿšāʾ ben-ʾăḥîyāh lᵉḇêṯ-yiśāśḵār — Baasha son of Ahijah from the tribe of Issachar conspired (qāšar, H7194, to bind/conspire) against Nadab. The verb qāšar is the standard term for palace coups in Kings; its occurrence marks the fragile politics of the northern monarchy. Gibbethon was a Philistine-held town on the Dan–Ephraim border, where Israel was engaged in siege when the coup occurred — the army was away from the capital.
15.28: wayyamîṯēhû baʿšāʾ bišnat šālōš lᵉʾāsāʾ — Baasha killed him in Asa's third year and reigned in his place. The dynastic shift from Jeroboam's house to Baasha's is complete in a single verse. The brutal efficiency of the coup — striking during an active siege — is a realistic political-military detail.
15.29: wayyᵉhî kᵉmalḵô hikkāh ʾeṯ-kol-bêṯ yārāḇʿām lōʾ hišʾîr kol-nᵉšāmāh lᵉyārāḇʿām — as soon as he became king, he struck down the entire house of Jeroboam, leaving not one soul (nᵉšāmāh, H5397). The term nᵉšāmāh (breath/soul/living being) here means 'a single living person' — total dynastic elimination. The hiphil of šāʾar (leave behind) with lōʾ is the standard formula for total annihilation of a royal house (cf. 16:11).
15.30: ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾṯ yārāḇʿām ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ waʾăšer heḥᵉṭîʾ ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl — the theological rationale: because of the sins Jeroboam sinned and caused Israel to sin — the double accusation (ḥāṭāʾ and heḥᵉṭîʾ hiphil) marks both personal guilt and corporate causation. The phrase bᵉkaʿᵃsô ʾăšer hiḵʿîs ʾeṯ-YHWH — by the provocation with which he provoked YHWH — introduces kaʿas (H3708, vexation/anger-provocation), a term for idolatry-induced divine anger.
15.31: The archival closing formula for Nadab: wᵉyeter diḇrê nāḏāḇ wᵉḵol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh — the rest of Nadab's acts and all he did are written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The reference to a northern chronicles document parallels the Judean source in v.7; both are now lost but imply court records underlying the Deuteronomistic account.
15.32: Resumptive war notice: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-ʾāsāʾ ûḇên-baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl kol-yᵉmêhem — war between Asa and Baasha all their days. This repetition of v.16 brackets the Nadab episode and resumes the Asa–Baasha narrative thread. Repetitive resumption is a characteristic feature of the Deuteronomistic compositional technique.
15.33: bišnat šālōš lᵉʾāsāʾ melek yᵉhûḏāh mālaḵ baʿšāʾ ben-ʾăḥîyāh ʿal-kol-yiśrāʾēl — Baasha's accession in Asa's third year at Tirzah (ṯirṣāh), ruling the full (kol) Israel. The 24-year reign is the longest of any non-Omride northern king. Tirzah (modern Tell el-Farah North) served as the northern capital until Omri founded Samaria in 16:24.
15.34: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyēleḵ bᵉḏereḵ yārāḇʿām — the same Jeroboamic evaluation formula applied to Baasha. The irony is sharp: Baasha was raised up to judge Jeroboam's house (16:2–3) but walks in the same sin he was sent to punish. This pattern — instrument of judgment becoming the next subject of judgment — is a Deuteronomistic theological motif.

16.13: The explanation for the fall of Baasha’s dynasty names the instrument of provocation as hāḇlêhem — ‘their idols,’ literally ‘their vapors/vanities.’ The noun heḇel (breath, vapor, vanity) is the OT’s theological critique of idolatry in one word: idols are as insubstantial and transient as a breath of air. Heḇel is the keynote word of Qohelet: heḇel hăḇālîm (Eccl 1:2, ‘vanity of vanities’) — the same root used there for all human striving under the sun without the fear of God. When the OT describes idols as hăḇālîm, it places them in the same category as the futile: things that exist but have no solid being, no life-giving capacity, no rûaḥ (breath/spirit) of their own. Jer 10:15: heḇel hēmmāh maʿăśēh taʿtuʿîm — ‘they are vanity, a work of delusion.’ The LXX translates heḇel as mataios (vain, futile), which Paul uses in Acts 14:15 (‘turn from these vain things, tōn mataion, to a living God’) and in Rom 1:21 (‘they became futile, emataiōthēsan, in their thinking’). The OT’s heḇel-critique of idolatry is the precise theological vocabulary on which Paul builds his argument in Rom 1:18-25: to exchange the glory of the incorruptible God for an image is to exchange substance for vapor.
16.1: wayᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾel-yēhûʾ ben-ḥănānî ʾel-baʿšāʾ — the word of YHWH came to Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha. The prophetic messenger formula (dᵉḇar-YHWH hāyāh ʾel) marks this as a formal oracle. Jehu son of Hanani is a northern prophet; his father Hanani appears in 2 Chr 16:7 as the seer who rebuked Asa. The oracle against Baasha mirrors the oracle against Jeroboam (14:1–18).
16.2: yaʿan ʾăšer hărîmōṯîḵā min-hāʾāpār — because I lifted you from the dust. The elevation-from-nothing formula (min-hāʾāpār, from the dust) echoes 1 Sam 2:8 and reflects the standard covenant-grant language: the sovereign elevates a vassal from obscurity and places him over the people. Baasha's failure to reciprocate this patronage with covenant loyalty is the charge.
16.3: hinnᵉnî mᵉbaʿer ʾaḥărê baʿšāʾ wᵉʾaḥărê bêṯô — I am about to sweep away (bāʿar, H1197, to burn/sweep/consume) after Baasha and his house. The same verb was used against Jeroboam (14:10) and will recur for Ahab (21:21), creating a theological pattern: each dynasty that walks in Jeroboam's sin receives the same consuming judgment. Wᵉnāṯattî bêṯᵉḵā kᵉḇêṯ yārāḇʿām ben-nᵉḇāṭ — I will make your house like the house of Jeroboam.
16.4: hammēṯ lᵉḇaʿšāʾ bāʿîr yōʾḵᵉlû haqqᵉlāḇîm — the one who dies in the city, the dogs will eat him; the one who dies in the field, the birds of the air will eat him. This curse formula (also in 14:11 for Jeroboam's house) denies proper burial — one of the worst fates in the ANE honor-shame economy. Proper burial (qᵉḇûrāh) was essential for posthumous honor; dogs and birds consuming the corpse represented maximal disgrace.
16.5: The archival closing formula for Baasha: wᵉyeter diḇrê baʿšāʾ wʾăšer ʿāśāh ûgᵉḇûrāṯô — the rest of Baasha's acts and all he did and his might are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The addition of gᵉḇûrāṯô (his might/valor) suggests military exploits recorded elsewhere; the canonical account focuses on the theological verdict.
16.6: wayyiškaḇ baʿšāʾ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqqāḇēr bᵉṯirṣāh — Baasha rested with his ancestors and was buried at Tirzah. Despite the oracle against his house (v.3–4), Baasha himself receives the conventional 'rest with ancestors' and burial — the dynastic elimination falls on his son Elah. His son Elah reigned in his place: the taḥtāyw succession formula maintains narrative continuity even for a doomed dynasty.
16.7: wᵉgam bᵉyad-yēhûʾ ben-ḥănānî hānāḇîʾ hāyāh dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾel-baʿšāʾ wᵉʾel-bêṯô — and also through Jehu son of Hanani the prophet, the word of YHWH came against Baasha and his house — the double oracle structure: the prophetic word is both the narrative explanation (v.1–4) and here confirmed as having come through the prophet. The clause ʿal-kol-hārāʿāh ʾăšer ʿāśāh (for all the evil he did) adds the specific charge of provoking YHWH to anger with the work of his hands.
16.8: bišnat ʿeśrîm wᵉšēš lᵉʾāsāʾ melek yᵉhûḏāh mālaḵ ʾēlāh ben-baʿšāʾ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl bᵉṯirṣāh šᵉnātayim — Elah son of Baasha became king of Israel in Asa's twenty-sixth year, reigning two years at Tirzah. The two-year reign is the minimum before conspiracy overtakes him. His reign contains no evaluative formula — the historian reserves comment until the conspiracy narrative makes the theological point implicit.
16.9: wayyiqšor ʿālāyw zimrî śar maḥᵃṣît harreḵeḇ — Zimri, commander of half his chariot force (maḥᵃṣît harreḵeḇ — half the chariotry), conspired against Elah. The rank marks Zimri as a senior military officer, not a common soldier. The conspiracy occurs while Elah was drinking himself drunk (šāṯāh šikḵôr) in the house of Arza, steward of the palace at Tirzah — the detail of vulnerability (drunkenness, location away from armed guard) explains the coup's success.
16.10: wayyāḇōʾ zimrî wayyakkēhû wayyᵉmîṯēhû bišnat ʿeśrîm wᵉšeḇaʿ lᵉʾāsāʾ — Zimri came in, struck him, and killed him in Asa's twenty-seventh year, and reigned in his place. The narrative's speed — three verbs, three actions — mirrors the coup's summary brutality. Zimri's reign begins and will end within seven days (v.15).
16.11: wayyᵉhî bᵉmalḵô kᵉšiḇtô ʿal-kissᵉʾô hikkāh ʾeṯ-kol-bêṯ baʿšāʾ lōʾ-hišʾîr lô maštin bᵉqîr — as soon as he sat on the throne, he struck down all the house of Baasha; he left not one male (maštin bᵉqîr, literally 'one urinating against a wall' — a coarse idiom for any male). This phrase (also in 14:10; 21:21; 1 Sam 25:22) is a Deuteronomistic formula for total male elimination. Wᵉgōʾălāyw wᵉmērēʿēhû — his relatives and friends (blood-avengers and allies) — are also eliminated, cutting off any future revenge claim.
16.12: wayyašmēd zimrî ʾēṯ kol-bêṯ baʿšāʾ kᵉḏiḇar YHWH ʾăšer dibber ʾel-baʿšāʾ bᵉyad yēhûʾ hannāḇîʾ — Zimri destroyed the entire house of Baasha in fulfillment of the word of YHWH that Jehu the prophet had spoken against Baasha. The fulfillment formula (kᵉḏiḇar YHWH ʾăšer dibber) closes the prophetic oracle that opened in v.1–4. Even a seven-day usurper can be the instrument of divine prophetic fulfillment — the theology does not require moral worthiness in the instrument.
16.14: Closing formula for Elah: wᵉyeter diḇrê ʾēlāh wᵉḵol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh — in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The brevity of the Elah entry is consistent with the narrative's focus on the theological pattern rather than the historical details of short, failed reigns.
16.15: bišnat ʿeśrîm wᵉšeḇaʿ lᵉʾāsāʾ mālaḵ zimrî šiḇʿat yāmîm bᵉṯirṣāh — Zimri reigned seven days at Tirzah in Asa's twenty-seventh year. The seven-day duration is the shortest royal tenure in the Kings narrative, a record of failure. The army (hāʿām, the people/troops) was encamped against Gibbethon of the Philistines — the same siege at which Baasha killed Nadab (15:27), now resumed or still ongoing.
16.16: wayyišmᵉʿû hāʿām hahōnîm lēʾmōr qāšar zimrî wᵉgam hikkāh ʾeṯ-hammelek — the encamped troops heard that Zimri had conspired and had also killed the king, and all Israel made Omri the army commander king that day at the camp. The field election — wayyamlîḵû ʾeṯ-ʿomrî śar-haṣṣāḇāʾ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl bayyôm hahûʾ bammaḥăneh — is an improvised military acclamation, not a formal succession.
16.17: wayyaʿal ʿomrî wᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô miggibbeṯôn wayyāṣurû ʿal-ṯirṣāh — Omri marched up from Gibbethon with all Israel and besieged Tirzah. The siege of the capital by the army against Zimri is a civil war in microcosm: the field army vs. the recently established palace coup. The verb ṣûr (to besiege) marks Tirzah as an enemy city within Israel's own territory.
16.18: wayyᵉhî kirʾōṯ zimrî kî-nilkᵉḏāh hāʿîr wayyāḇōʾ ʾel-bîraṯ bêṯ-hammelek wayyiśrōp ʿālāyw ʾeṯ-bêṯ-hammelek bāʾēš wayyāmāṯ — when Zimri saw that the city was captured, he went into the inner citadel (bîrāh, H1002) of the palace and burned the palace over himself (wayyiśrōp ʿālāyw) and died. Suicide by palace arson — a defiant final act that deprives his conqueror of both a captive and a trophy; the palace becomes his funeral pyre.
16.19: ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾṯāyw ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ laʿăśôṯ hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH — Zimri's death is given the standard theological verdict despite the seven-day reign; he walked in the way of Jeroboam and in the sins by which Jeroboam caused Israel to sin. The Deuteronomistic formula operates independently of duration — even a seven-day king who followed the founding apostasy receives the Jeroboamic verdict.
16.20: The archival reference for Zimri: wᵉyeter diḇrê zimrî wᵉqišrô ʾăšer qāšar — the rest of Zimri's acts and his conspiracy that he conspired are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The closing formula here uniquely adds the conspiracy detail (qišrô ʾăšer qāšar) — the conspiracy is the defining act of a seven-day reign with nothing else to record.
16.21: ʾāz yēḥāleq hāʿām yiśrāʾēl laḥᵃṣî — then the people of Israel were divided in half. The split is between Tibni son of Ginath (backed by half the population) and Omri (backed by the other half). The civil war between these two factions parallels the earlier north-south split; within the northern kingdom, the military/Omride faction fights a civilian/Ginath faction for four years (cf. v.23's dating).
16.22: wayyeḥᵉzaq hāʿām ʾăšer ʾaḥărê ʿomrî ʾeṯ-hāʿām ʾăšer ʾaḥărê ṯiḇnî — the people behind Omri prevailed over the people behind Tibni. Wayyāmāṯ ṯiḇnî — Tibni died, leaving the cause of his faction. The cause of death is not stated; the text is indifferent to the manner, only the result. Omri was king — the terse accession formula closes the civil war narrative.
16.23: bišnat šᵉlōšîm wᵉʾaḥat lᵉʾāsāʾ melek yᵉhûḏāh mālaḵ ʿomrî ʾel-yiśrāʾēl šᵉtêm ʿeśrēh šānāh — Omri became king in Asa's thirty-first year; he reigned twelve years. Six years in Tirzah (ṯirṣāh) then moved to Samaria. The twelve-year figure and the six-at-Tirzah note create a chronological precision that anchors the Omride dynasty in the synchronistic system.
16.24: wayyiqen ʾeṯ-hār-šōmᵉrôn mēʾēṯ-šemer bišᵉnê kikkārim-kesef — he bought the hill of Samaria (har-šōmᵉrôn) from Shemer for two talents of silver. The Hebrew etymology is explicit: the city name Šōmᵉrôn derives from the seller's name Šemer — Omri named the city after the former owner of the hill, an unusual naming convention that may preserve legal or honorific memory of the transaction. Two talents of silver (about 68 kg) for a strategic hill — a significant but not extraordinary price for a royal land purchase.
16.25: wayyaʿaś ʿomrî hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyāreʿ mikkōl ʾăšer lᵉpānāyw — Omri did more evil than all before him. The comparative formula wayyāreʿ mikkōl (did worse than all) is escalating: each northern dynasty receives a slightly worse verdict than the last, building to Ahab's climactic judgment in v.30–33. The Deuteronomistic theology of progressive corruption uses this comparative rhetoric to explain the eventual Assyrian destruction.
16.26: wayyēleḵ bᵉḵol-dereḵ yārāḇʿām ben-nᵉḇāṭ ûḇᵉḥaṭṭōʾṯāyw ʾăšer heḥᵉṭîʾ ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl — Omri walked fully in the way of Jeroboam and in the sins by which Jeroboam caused Israel to sin, provoking YHWH the God of Israel to anger with their haḇlêhem (H1892, vapors/vanities/idols — the same word used in Ecclesiastes for futility). Haḇlîm is a secondary pejorative for idols, implying their insubstantiality.
16.27: Archival formula for Omri: wᵉyeter diḇrê ʿomrî ʾăšer ʿāśāh ûgᵉḇûrāṯô ʾăšer gāḇar — the rest of Omri's acts and the strength he showed are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The Assyrian records call Israel 'the house of Omri' (bît-ḥumrî) for well over a century after his dynasty ended, indicating Omri's actual historical significance far exceeds what the Deuteronomistic account allots him — the theological evaluation has compressed a politically major reign.
16.28: wayyiškaḇ ʿomrî ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqqāḇēr bᵉšōmᵉrôn — Omri rested with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria — the first king buried in his own newly founded capital rather than an ancestral city. His son Ahab reigned in his place: the succession formula introduces the figure who will dominate the next 10 chapters.
16.29: wᵉʾaḥāḇ ben-ʿomrî mālaḵ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl bišnat šᵉlōšîm wᵉšᵉmōneh šānāh lᵉʾāsāʾ — Ahab son of Omri became king in Asa's thirty-eighth year, reigning 22 years. The synchronistic dating places him deep within the long Asa reign. The narrative will devote more space to Ahab than to any other northern king — not for his political achievements but for the religious confrontation with Elijah.
16.30: wayyaʿaś ʾaḥāḇ ben-ʿomrî hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH mikkōl ʾăšer lᵉpānāyw — Ahab did more evil than all before him, continuing the escalating comparative formula from v.25. The historian uses the superlative not for hyperbole but as theological periodization: Ahab represents the culmination of northern apostasy, requiring the extraordinary prophetic response of Elijah and Elisha.
16.31: wayyᵉhî hănaqlāh lekteṯ bᵉḥaṭṭōʾṯ yārāḇʿām ben-nᵉḇāṭ wayyiqqaḥ ʾišṯāh ʾeṯ-ʾîzeḇel bat-ʾeṯbaʿal melek ṣîḏōnîm — as if it were a light thing (rhetorically ironic hănaqlāh construction), he took to wife Jezebel daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians. ʾîzeḇel (H348) — the etymology is debated: possibly 'where is the prince?' (a cultic mourning formula for Baal) or 'Baal is exalted.' ʾeṯbaʿal ('with-Baal' or 'Baal lives') identifies her father as a Phoenician king with explicit Baal devotion. The marriage brings Baal worship into the northern royal court as a state-sponsored institution.
16.32: wayyāqem mizbēaḥ labbāʿal bêṯ habbāʿal ʾăšer bānāh bᵉšōmᵉrôn — he set up an altar for Baal in the temple of Baal that he built in Samaria. The construction of a royal Baal temple in the capital — distinct from the golden-calf shrines at Bethel and Dan (Jeroboam's sin) — represents a qualitative escalation: now foreign Phoenician Baal (not an altered YHWH cult) is institutionalized in the capital. This is the specific act that distinguishes Ahab's apostasy from all predecessors.
16.33: wayyaʿaś ʾaḥāḇ ʾeṯ-hāʾăšērāh — Ahab also made the Asherah pole (ʾăšērāh, H842 — both the goddess and her sacred wooden symbol). Wayyōsep ʾaḥāḇ laʿăśôṯ lᵉhaḵʿîs ʾeṯ-YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl mikkōl malkê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer hāyû lᵉpānāyw — Ahab did more to provoke YHWH than all the kings before him — the absolute superlative closes the escalating series (vv.25, 30, 33), establishing Ahab as the nadir of northern kingship and the necessary context for Elijah's ministry.
16.34: bᵉyāmāyw bānāh ḥîʾēl habbêṯᵉʾēlî ʾeṯ-yᵉrîḥōh — in his days Hiel the Bethelite rebuilt Jericho. He laid its foundation at the cost of Abiram his firstborn and set up its gates at the cost of Segub his youngest son — bᵉʾăḇîrām bᵉḵōrô yaṣṣîḇ ûḇîśgûḇ ṣᵉʿîrô nîṣṣaḇ dᵉlāṯêyhā. The fulfillment of Joshua's curse (Josh 6:26: 'at the cost of his firstborn he shall lay its foundation, and at the cost of his youngest son he shall set up its gates') — the vocabulary of Joshua and 1 Kings 16:34 are verbally identical. Whether the deaths were coincidental or literal fulfillment of the oath, the historian presents them as prophetic completion: the Elijah narrative will open immediately after this notice, connecting Joshua's ancient word to the new prophetic era.

17.1: Elijah’s drought announcement opens with the formula: ḥay YHWH ʾelōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — ‘As YHWH the God of Israel lives, before whom I stand (lᵉpānāyw — to/before his face).’ The phrase ʿāmad lᵉpānāyw (stand before him) is the formula of prophetic investiture: the one who stands before YHWH has access to the divine council (sôḏ YHWH) and delivers its decrees as the divine messenger. Jer 23:18 makes this the mark of true prophecy: ‘for who among them has stood in the council of YHWH (baʿăṣat YHWH) to see and to hear his word?’ The false prophet speaks without standing; the true prophet speaks from standing before YHWH. The same formula describes the seraphim (‘standing above him,’ Isa 6:2), the angelic court in Job 1:6, and the Levitical ministry (‘to stand before YHWH to serve him,’ Deut 10:8). Heb 7:25 applies the standing-before-God formula to Christ’s perpetual intercession: ‘he always lives to make intercession for them’ — his permanent lᵉpānāyw presence before the Father is the fulfillment of what the prophet’s standing before YHWH anticipated, now without intermission and without the distance of messenger-relationship.
17.21: Elijah’s prayer over the widow’s dead son uses an unusual verb: wayyitmōdad ʿal hayyelεḏ šālōš pᵉʿāmîm — ‘he stretched/measured himself upon the child three times.’ The verb mādad (to measure) in the hitpael (reflexive/intensive) means ‘to stretch oneself out measuring against’ — a physical alignment of the prophet’s living body against the child’s dead body, repeated three times. The three-fold repetition (šālōš pᵉʿāmîm) marks intensity of intercessory prayer rather than mechanical technique: the life that returns comes from Elijah’s prayer (v21b: wayyiqrāʾ ʾel YHWH, ‘he cried to YHWH’), not from the physical posture alone. The Elisha parallel (2 Kgs 4:34: wayyiggahar ʿālaw, ‘he stretched himself over him’) establishes this as a prophetic pattern: physical closeness of the life-bearing prophet to the dead child as the vehicle of divine life-transfer. Paul over Eutychus (Acts 20:10: epipesōn autō, fell upon him) continues the pattern in apostolic form. The underlying theological claim in all three instances is that life-giving is a divine prerogative mediated through the prophet/apostle’s bodily and intercessory presence — a pattern whose ultimate ground is the incarnation: the Life of the world (John 11:25) taking on a body in order to give life through physical presence and sacrificial death.
17.2: wayᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr — the word of YHWH came to him, saying. The divine-word reception formula occurs three times in ch.17 (vv.2, 8, 14/the oracle form), structuring the chapter as three successive divine directions. The brevity of v.2 — no elaboration, just the formula — intensifies the sense of immediate divine command after Elijah's drought announcement.
17.3: leḵ-lᵉḵā miṭṭāh wᵉp̄ānîṯā lᵉḵā qēḏmāh wᵉnistaraṯṯā bᵉnaḥal kᵉrîṯ — go from here, turn east, and hide yourself by the brook Cherith. Nistaraṯṭā (hithpael of sātar, to hide/conceal) — the command to hiddenness is theologically significant: Elijah disappears from Ahab's reach at YHWH's direction, not from fear. Cherith (kᵉrîṯ) east of the Jordan remains unidentified; the Jordan crossing removes Elijah from Ahab's jurisdiction entirely.
17.4: wᵉhāyāh min-hannaḥal tišṯeh wᵉʾeṯ-hāʿōrᵉḇîm ṣiwwîṯî lᵉkalkēlkāh šām — you will drink from the brook, and I have commanded (ṣiwwîṯî, perfect — already commanded) the ravens (hāʿōrᵉḇîm, H6158) to feed you there. The ravens were ritually unclean birds (Lev 11:15) — their use as divine provision agents subverts purity categories to serve prophetic sustenance. The perfect tense of the divine command (ṣiwwîṯî) treats the instruction as already accomplished before Elijah moves.
17.5: wayyēleḵ wayyaʿaś kidbar YHWH — he went and did according to the word of YHWH. The obedience report uses the exact formula of Abrahamic compliance (Gen 22:3 uses the same pattern). Wayyēšeḇ bᵉnaḥal kᵉrîṯ — he settled (yāšaḇ) by the brook Cherith — not a temporary stop but an established dwelling, underscoring the months-long duration of the provision.
17.6: wᵉhāʿōrᵉḇîm mᵉḇîʾîm lô leḥem ûḇāśār babbōqer wᵉleḥem ûḇāśār bāʿereḇ — the ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning and bread and meat in the evening. The double provision — morning and evening — mirrors the manna pattern (Exod 16:8, 12–13) and the Passover timing, connecting Elijah's sustenance to the wilderness provision of the Mosaic period. The source of the ravens' bread and meat is unspecified — the text focuses on the act of provision, not its logistics.
17.7: wayyᵉhî miqṣēh yāmîm wayyiḇaš hannaḥal kî lōʾ-hāyāh gešem bāʾāreṣ — after some time the brook dried up because there was no rain in the land. The phrase miqṣēh yāmîm (after the end of days) is deliberately vague — the drought was progressive, not instantaneous. The drying of Cherith forces the next divine direction; YHWH's provision does not insulate Elijah from the drought's effects but redirects him through them.
17.8: wayyᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr — the second occurrence of the divine-word formula in ch.17 (cf. v.2). Its repetition at the moment of crisis (the dried brook) signals that YHWH's direction is responsive to changed circumstances, not static. The prophet is not left without instruction when his provision source fails.
17.9: qûm leḵ ṣārᵉpāṯāh ʾăšer lᵉṣîḏôn wᵉyāšaḇtā šām — go to Zarephath which belongs to Sidon and live there. Zarephath (ṣārᵉpaṯ, H6886 — modern Sarafand in Lebanon) was in Phoenician territory, the homeland of Jezebel's father Ethbaal. The theological irony: YHWH sends his prophet to the pagan widow in the land of Baal worship, not to any of Israel's many widows (Luke 4:25–26 makes this explicit). Ḥinnēh ṣiwwîṯî šām ʾišāh ʾalmānāh lᵉḵalkᵉlāk — I have commanded a widow there to feed you. The divine ṣiwwîṯî perfect reappears: the provision is already arranged before Elijah arrives.
17.10: wayyāqom wayyēleḵ ṣārᵉpāṯāh — Elijah rose and went to Zarephath. The obedience formula echoes v.5. At the city gate (pēṯaḥ hāʿîr) he finds the widow gathering sticks (mᵉqoššeš ʿēṣîm). Wayyiqrāʾ ʾēleyhā wayyōʾmar qᵉḥî-nāʾ lî mᵉʿaṭ-mayim bakkᵉlî wᵉʾešṯeh — he called to her and asked for a little water in a vessel to drink. The request begins with the least — water — before escalating to food.
17.11: wattēleḵ lāqaḥat wayyiqrāʾ ʾēleyhā wayyōʾmar qᵉḥî-nāʾ lî paṯ-leḥem bᵉyāḏēḵ — as she went to get it, he called again and asked for a piece of bread as well. The escalation of request tests the widow's hospitality. Her immediate compliance with the water request but the pause before bread (she must explain her situation in v.12) reveals the text's narrative pacing: the water was easy to give; the bread will require a miracle.
17.12: ḥay YHWH ʾĕlōhayiḵ ʾim-yeš-lî māṣôg — as YHWH your God lives, I have nothing baked. The widow's oath formula invokes YHWH ʾĕlōhayiḵ — YHWH your God (not 'my God' — she is Phoenician, outside the covenant). She swears by the prophet's God, acknowledging his deity's existence without personal covenant membership. Her situation: a handful of flour in a jar (kap-qemeḥ bakkad) and a little oil in a jug (mᵉʿaṭ-šemen baṣṣappāḥaṯ) — the minimum for one final meal before death by starvation.
17.13: wayyōʾmer ʾēleyhā ʾēlîyāhû ʾal-tîrʾî — Elijah said to her, do not be afraid. The prophetic reassurance formula (ʾal-tîrʾî) before the miracle command recurs throughout the prophetic narratives (cf. 2 Kgs 4:8–37). ʿăśî ḵaddāḇār ʾaḵ ʿăśî-lî mišṣām ʾūgāh qᵉṭannāh bāriʾšōnāh — but first make me a small cake from it and bring it out. The 'first-for-me' command requires priority hospitality to the prophet, testing the depth of her compliance with the divine commission to feed him (v.9).
17.14: kî-ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl kad-qemeḥ lōʾ tiḵleh wᵉṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen lōʾ tiḥsar — for thus says YHWH the God of Israel: the jar of flour will not be used up (lōʾ tiḵleh, will not be exhausted) and the jug of oil will not run dry (lōʾ tiḥsar, will not lack/fail). The divine oracle guarantees the miracle in advance of compliance. The two negatives with lōʾ + imperfect express permanent ongoing supply, not a one-time refill. ʿAḏ yôm tēṯ-YHWH gešem ʿal-pᵉnê hāʾădāmāh — until the day YHWH sends rain on the face of the earth — the miracle is temporally bounded by the drought oracle, connecting Zarephath provision to the Carmel confrontation to come.
17.15: wattēleḵ wattaʿaś kidbar ʾēlîyāhû — she went and did according to Elijah's word. The obedience formula applies now to the widow, paralleling the formula used for Elijah (v.5). Wattōʾkal hîʾ wᵉhûʾ ûḇêṯāh yāmîm — she and Elijah and her household ate for many days. The tripartite beneficiaries (widow, prophet, household) and the open-ended duration (yāmîm, days — many days) indicate the miracle was sustained provision, not a single instance.
17.16: kad-qemeḥ lōʾ-ḵālāṯāh wᵉṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen lōʾ-ḥāsēr kidbar YHWH ʾăšer dibber bᵉyad ʾēlîyāhû — the jar of flour was not exhausted and the jug of oil did not fail, according to the word of YHWH that he had spoken through Elijah. The fulfillment report mirrors the oracle of v.14 with verbal precision — the same nouns (kad-qemeḥ, ṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen), the same negated verbs (lōʾ ḵālāṯāh, lōʾ ḥāsēr). The fulfillment formula (kidbar YHWH ʾăšer dibber bᵉyad) marks the miracle as prophetic-word confirmation.
17.17: wayyᵉhî ʾaḥar haddᵉḇārîm hāʾēlleh ḥālāh ben-hāʾišāh baʿălaṯ habbāyiṯ — after these things the son of the woman who owned the house became ill. Wayyᵉhî ḥolyô ḥāzāq mᵉʾōḏ ʿaḏ ʾăšer lōʾ-nôṯᵃrāh bô nᵉšāmāh — his illness was so severe that no breath (nᵉšāmāh, H5397) remained in him. The term nᵉšāmāh (breath of life, the animating principle from Gen 2:7) is used here for its cessation — the child is clinically dead, not merely ill. The narrative escalation from provision miracle to resurrection sets up the climactic encounter.
17.18: wattōʾmer ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû mah-lî wālaḵ ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — she said to Elijah, what is between me and you, O man of God? The formula mah-lî wālaḵ (what to me and to you?) is an idiom of social distancing, expressing that the relationship has produced an unwanted outcome. Hābāʾṯā ʾēlay lᵉhazḵîr ʾeṯ-ʿăwōnî ûlᵉhamîṯ ʾeṯ-bᵉnî — have you come to call my sin to remembrance and to kill my son? She interprets the death as divine punishment triggered by the prophet's presence — a recognition that proximity to a holy man makes sin visible before God. The theology of divine holiness exposing human sin through prophetic presence is consistent with the Deuteronomistic framework.
17.19: wayyōʾmer ʾēleyhā tᵉnî-lî ʾeṯ-bᵉnēḵ wayyiqqāḥēhû mibᵉḥêqāh — give me your son. He took him from her arms (mibᵉḥêqāh, from her bosom/arms — the lap-embrace of nursing or mourning). Wayyaʿălēhû ʾel-hāʿălîyyāh ʾăšer-hûʾ yōšēḇ šām wayyaśkîḇēhû ʾal-miṭṭāṯô — he carried him up to the upper room where he was staying and laid him on his own bed. The upper room (ʿălîyyāh, H5944) is the private chamber of the guest; laying the child on the prophet's own bed enacts a transfer of prophetic life-force or intimate intercession.
17.20: wayyiqrāʾ ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmar YHWH ʾĕlōhay hăgam ʿal-hāʾalmānāh ʾăšer-ʾănî miṯgôrēr ʿimmāh hārēʿōṯā lᵉhamîṯ ʾeṯ-bᵉnāh — he cried to YHWH: YHWH my God, have you also brought evil upon the widow with whom I am staying, to kill her son? Miṯgôrēr (hithpael of gûr, to sojourn) — Elijah presents himself as a dependent guest; the death of his host's son would bring reproach on his prophetic mission. The prayer is a bold challenge to divine action, consistent with the prophetic tradition of complaint-petition (cf. Moses, Habakkuk).
17.22: wayyišmaʿ YHWH bᵉqôl ʾēlîyāhû wattāšāḇ nefesh-hayyeled ʿal-qirḇô wayyeḥî — YHWH heard Elijah's voice, and the soul of the child (nefesh-hayyeled) returned to his inward parts (ʿal-qirḇô), and he revived (wayyeḥî). The term nefesh (H5315) returns to the body (qereb, H7130, the inner organs/interior) — the resurrection is anatomically described as the animating principle re-inhabiting the physical body. This is the first recorded resurrection in Scripture; the language of nefesh-return will recur in 2 Kgs 4:34–35 (Elisha) and Ezek 37 (valley of dry bones).
17.23: wayyiqqaḥ ʾēlîyāhû ʾeṯ-hayyeled wayyōrîḏēhû min-hāʿălîyyāh habbayᵉṯāh wayyittᵉnēhû lᵉʾimmô — Elijah took the child and brought him down from the upper room into the house and gave him to his mother. The descent from the upper room mirrors the ascent of v.19 — the spatial movement (up for death/prayer, down for restoration) enacts the reversal. Wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû rᵉʾî ḥay bᵉnēḵ — Elijah said, see, your son lives. The announcement is direct and without elaboration: ḥay (alive), confirmed by presentation.
17.24: wattōʾmer hāʾišāh ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû ʿattāh zeh yāḏaʿtî kî ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh — the woman said, now I know that you are a man of God (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm). The confession of the Sidonian widow mirrors the structural logic of the prophetic sign: the miracle authenticates the messenger. Ûḏᵉḇar-YHWH bᵉpîḵā ʾĕmeṯ — and the word of YHWH in your mouth is truth (ʾĕmeṯ, H571, faithfulness/reliability). The widow's conclusion is twofold: the prophet is genuine and YHWH's word is ʾĕmeṯ — the latter term carries the sense of durable reliability, not just factual accuracy. A Phoenician woman outside the covenant becomes a witness to YHWH's prophetic truthfulness.

1.1: wĕhamelek Dāwîḏ zāqēn bā bayāmîm wĕlō yiḥam lô: 'And king David was old, advanced in days, and he could not get warm.' Two phrases mark the narrative's opening: zāqēn bā bayāmîm (old, come in days) — the standard biblical idiom for advanced age (Gen 18:11: Abraham and Sarah; Gen 24:1: Abraham alone; Josh 13:1; 23:1-2: Joshua) — and the verb yiḥam (Qal imperfect of ḥmm, to be warm), here negated: the king can no longer maintain body heat. This is the standard biblical onset-of-death marker; its significance is immediately political: a king who cannot maintain physical warmth cannot exercise executive authority.
1.2: naʿărâ ḇĕṯûlâ: 'a young woman, a virgin.' The two terms are distinct: naʿărâ describes a young woman of marriageable age (the same word used of Rebekah in Gen 24 and Ruth gleaning in Ruth 2); ḇĕṯûlâ (H1330) specifies virginal status. The proposal is therapeutic — ANE medicine recognized the warming effect of body heat proximity for the elderly — not sexual. Verse 4 makes explicit that David 'did not know her' (wĕhammelek lō yĕḏāʿāh), a formulaic Hebrew idiom for no sexual intercourse (Gen 4:1: Adam 'knew' Eve; 1 Sam 1:19). The proposal comes from David's servants (ʿăḇāḏāyw), not from David himself.
1.3: wayĕḇaqqĕšû naʿărâ yāp̄â bĕḵōl gĕḇûl yiśrāʾēl: 'They searched for a beautiful young woman throughout all the territory of Israel.' The search is a national operation — kol gĕḇûl yiśrāʾēl (the whole border/territory of Israel) is a comprehensive formula for the entire kingdom. Šûnēm (Shunem) is in the tribal territory of Issachar, in the Valley of Jezreel — the same village where the Shunammite woman will live in 2 Kings 4 (Elisha raises her son). The geographic precision is characteristic of the court archive sources underlying 1-2 Kings.
1.4: wattihî lammmelek mĕšāreṯeṯ: 'she became his attendant/servant.' The verb šārat (Piel, to serve/minister) is used for the service of Levites in the sanctuary (Num 1:50) and for personal household service; it denotes a close attendant relationship, not a wife or concubine. The emphatic statement wĕhammelek lō yĕḏāʿāh legally and morally clarifies her status: Abishag is never David's wife or concubine. This distinction will have political consequences in ch 2, when Adonijah's request for Abishag is interpreted as a claim on the throne.
1.5: wayyiṯnasĕʾ ʾăḏōniyyāhû: 'Adonijah exalted himself.' The verb is Hitpael of nāśāʾ (H5375) — reflexive/intensive, 'he lifted himself up.' The same pattern is used for Absalom (2 Sam 15:1: wayyaʿaś lô ʾAḇšālôm reḵeḇ ûp̄ārāšîm, chariot and horsemen). The Absalom-Adonijah parallel is structural: the third and fourth sons both attempt to seize the throne through public display and faction-building while the father lives. ʾĂnî ʾemlōk: 'I will be king' — the declarative self-assertion before any divine or royal authorization, which is the narrative's definition of the illegitimate seizure.
1.6: wĕlō-ʿăṣāḇô ʾāḇîw miyyāmāyw lēʾmōr maddûaʿ kāḵâ ʿāśîṯā: 'And his father had not disciplined him at any time by saying, Why have you done this?' The verb ʿāṣaḇ (H6087) means to distress, grieve, or restrain — here it describes parental correction. The same parental failure appears in Eli (1 Sam 3:13: his sons did evil and he did not restrain them) and David himself with Amnon (2 Sam 13:21: David was angry but did not discipline him). The note that Adonijah was 'also very handsome in appearance' (gam-hûʾ ṭôḇ tōʾar mĕʾōḏ) echoes Absalom (2 Sam 14:25); external beauty without inner character creates the same trajectory.
1.7: wayyihyû dĕḇārāyw ʿim Yôʾāḇ ben-Ṣĕrûyâ wĕʿim ʾAḇyāṯār hakkōhēn: 'His agreements/words were with Joab son of Zeruiah and with Abiathar the priest.' Dĕḇārāyw (his words/matters) here means his political negotiations or compact — an agreement to support his claim. Both Joab and Abiathar are key figures: Joab has been David's military commander throughout the Samuel narrative but has resisted David's authority repeatedly (killing Abner, Amasa, Absalom against David's explicit wishes); Abiathar is the priest from Eli's line (1 Sam 22:20) who has served David since Nob. Their alignment with Adonijah represents the older guard's faction.
1.8: wĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ wĕŠimʿî wĕRēʿî wĕhaggibōrîm ʾăšer lĕḏāwîḏ: Those NOT with Adonijah. The counter-faction is significant: Zadok (priest from the Eleazar line, not Eli's Abiathar line), Benaiah (commander of the elite corps), Nathan the prophet (David's court prophet since 2 Sam 7), and the gibbōrîm (David's professional warriors). The Zadok-Abiathar division prefigures the resolution of 2:26-27: when Abiathar is expelled, Zadok alone remains, establishing the Zadokite priestly line that will serve the Jerusalem temple through the monarchy.
1.9: wayyzaḇaḥ ʾAḏōniyyāhû ṣōʾn ûḇāqār ûmĕrîʾ ʿim ʾeḇen hazzōḥeleṯ: 'Adonijah sacrificed sheep, oxen, and fattened cattle at the stone of Zoheleth.' The sacrificial feast (zāḇaḥ) near En-rogel (the spring at the junction of the Kidron and Hinnom valleys, southeast of Jerusalem, outside the city) is a political maneuver: the sacrificial feast would be interpreted as a covenant meal ratifying Adonijah's kingship among his supporters. The invited list is explicitly political: 'all the king's sons and all the men of Judah, the king's servants.'
1.10: wĕʾeṯ-Nāṯān hannaḇîʾ wĕʾeṯ-Bĕnāyāhû wĕʾeṯ-haggibbōrîm wĕʾeṯ-šĕlōmōh ʾāḥîw lōʾ qārāʾ: 'But Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, the mighty men, and Solomon his brother he did not invite.' The deliberate exclusion of these figures from the covenant meal is the political signal: Adonijah is constituting his faction and excluding the rival faction. The exclusion of Solomon specifically marks this as a succession move. The narrator names them here to highlight the symmetry: those excluded by Adonijah are exactly those who will execute Solomon's succession.
1.11: wayĕdabbēr Nāṯān ʾel-Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾem šĕlōmōh: Nathan goes first to Bathsheba. Nathan's stratagem (vv11-14) is political wisdom: approach the king through Bathsheba rather than directly, creating a personal and maternal stake. The phrase hălōʾ šāmaʿt ('have you not heard?') opens with the rhetorical question of urgent news. Nathan's characterization of Adonijah's rule as threatening to both Bathsheba and Solomon — 'that both you and Solomon will be counted as criminals (ḥaṭṭāʾîm)' — is the political leverage: she has a survival reason to act, not merely a promise to invoke.
1.12: wĕʿattâ lĕkî ʾîṣĕʿēḵ nā ʿēṣâ: 'Now let me give you counsel.' ʿĒṣâ (H6098, counsel, advice) is the key political wisdom term throughout Samuel-Kings (Ahithophel's ʿēṣâ in 2 Sam 16-17 was 'like the word of God'). Nathan's counsel is tactically sound: have Bathsheba make the appeal on personal grounds (the king's oath) and Nathan will follow to confirm. The two-witness structure (Deut 19:15: a matter must be established by two or three witnesses) gives the appeal maximum legal weight.
1.13: hălōʾ ʾattâ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek nišbaʿtā lăʾămaṯĕkā lēʾmōr kî šĕlōmōh binkā yimlōk ʾaḥărāy: 'Did you not, my lord the king, swear to your handmaid saying, Your son Solomon shall reign after me?' The oath invoked here has no prior narrative documentation in 2 Samuel — it is presupposed as historical fact. The formula nišbaʿtā (Niphal perfect of šāḇaʿ, to swear) is the standard covenant-oath verb. Bathsheba's self-designation as ʾămaṯĕkā (your female slave/handmaid) is the formal court address of petition — it invokes covenant obligation, not merely personal relationship.
1.14: hinnēh ʿôḏēḵ mĕḏabbereṯ šām ʿim-hammelek wĕʾanî ʾāḇôʾ ʾaḥărāyiḵ: 'Behold, while you are still speaking there with the king, I will come in after you.' Nathan's timing is precise — Bathsheba makes the initial petition, creating the emotional and legal grounds, and Nathan follows to confirm. The sequential structure (woman first, prophet second) maximizes the impact: a mother's personal stake, then prophetic confirmation. This is political strategy by those who believe they are acting in accordance with God's choice.
1.15: waṯābōʾ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾel-hammelek haḥăḏrâ: 'Bathsheba came to the king in the inner chamber.' The ḥeḏer (inner chamber) is the most private room — David is being tended by Abishag. The scene underscores David's physical diminishment: the king who was once the most dynamic military and political figure in Israel now lies in the inner chamber unable to keep warm. Bathsheba enters, prostrates herself (waṯiqqōḏ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek), and waits for him to ask her purpose.
1.16: waṯiqqōḏ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek: 'Bathsheba bowed and prostrated herself to the king.' The verb pair qāḏad (to bow the head) + šāḥâ (to prostrate oneself) is the royal obeisance formula — used of subjects before kings and of worshipers before YHWH. By contrast, when Bathsheba later comes before Solomon in v16 (ch 2:19), he rises and bows to her — a reversal that demonstrates Solomon's honor to his mother and the changed political reality.
1.17: wayĕdabbēr ʾaḏōnî ʾeloyhēkā YHWH ʾel-ʾămaṯĕkā lēʾmōr: The invocation of 'YHWH your God' (using the third person to refer to the Lord) in Bathsheba's petition elevates the claim from personal request to covenantal obligation. David's oath was sworn by YHWH (ḥay YHWH, 'as YHWH lives', v29 confirms this formula) — breaking it would be not merely political failure but covenant perjury. The appeal grounds Solomon's succession in divine promise, not human political maneuvering.
1.18: wĕʿattâ hinnēh ʾAḏōniyyāhû mālaḵ: 'And now, behold, Adonijah has become king.' The perfect tense mālaḵ — 'has become king,' presenting it as an accomplished fact rather than ongoing process — is the rhetorical framing of urgency. Bathsheba presents the succession as a fait accompli that David must reverse, not a plan he can prevent. The appeal to David's ignorance ('you did not know it') may be slightly overstated — the narrative does not say David was completely unaware of Adonijah's actions — but politically it grants David the face-saving option of acting without acknowledging that he had passively allowed the situation to develop.
1.19: wayyzaḇaḥ šôr ûmĕrîʾ wĕṣōʾn lārōḇ wayyiqrāʾ lĕḵol bĕnê hammmelek: 'He sacrificed oxen and fattened cattle and sheep in abundance and invited all the king's sons.' The repetition of the feast-detail from v9 is Bathsheba's report to David — the narrative deliberately repeats the information to show what David was being told. The phrase wĕlišĕlōmōh ʿaḇdĕkā lōʾ qārāʾ (and Solomon your servant he did not call) is the pivot of the petition: Solomon's exclusion is the evidence that the succession is being stolen, and it personalizes the abstract political claim.
1.20: wĕʾattâ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʿênê kol-yiśrāʾēl ʿāleykā lĕhaggîḏ lāhem mî yēšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʾaḥărāyw: 'And now, my lord the king, the eyes of all Israel are upon you, to tell them who will sit on the throne of my lord the king after him.' The phrase ʿênê kol-yiśrāʾēl ʿāleykā (the eyes of all Israel are upon you) is a political and covenantal pressure statement — the king has a public obligation to declare his succession. 'Eyes upon' is a standard idiom for expectant attention requiring response. The question is not merely dynastic but concerns the stability of the covenant community.
1.21: wĕhāyâ kĕšeḵeḇ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wĕhāyîṯî ʾanî ûḇĕnî šĕlōmōh ḥaṭṭāʾîm: 'And it will be, when my lord the king lies with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon will be regarded as criminals.' The idiom šāḵaḇ ʿim ʾăḇōṯāyw (to lie with one's fathers) is the standard death formula for Israelite kings throughout 1-2 Kings — it will appear dozens of times. Ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners/criminals) — those who have violated the law. Bathsheba frames the political danger in the sharpest terms: if Solomon is passed over, she and Solomon will be treated as rebels against the new king.
1.22: wĕhinnēh ʿôḏennāh mĕḏabbereṯ ʿim-hammelek wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ bāʾ: 'And while she was still speaking with the king, Nathan the prophet came.' The narrative timing — Nathan arrives precisely while Bathsheba is speaking — is either coordinated (as Nathan told her in v14) or presented as providentially timed. The two-witness structure is completed: Bathsheba makes the petition, Nathan arrives to confirm it. The narrator uses the same phrasing as Nathan's instruction to create a ring structure confirming the plan worked exactly as devised.
1.23: wayuggad lammmelek lēʾmōr hinnēh Nāṯān hannaḇîʾ: Nathan is formally announced to the king — the court announcement protocol. His entry after Bathsheba follows the sequence Nathan prescribed; his presence confirms that both advisors are aligned on the matter. The parallel announcement formulas (Bathsheba prostrating in v16, Nathan prostrating in v23 with wayyišṯaḥû lammmelek ʾappāyw ʾarṣâ) create a literary balance: two witnesses, two prostrations, one message.
1.24: wayyōʾmer Nāṯān ʾăḏōnî hammelek ʾattâ āmartā ʾAḏōniyyāhû yimlōk ʾaḥărāy wĕhûʾ yēšēḇ ʿal-kissĕʾî: Nathan's approach is indirect — he does not say 'you promised Solomon.' Instead he asks: 'Did you say Adonijah shall reign?' This rhetorical strategy forces David to either deny or affirm, without Nathan presuming to accuse the king. The rhetorical question (ʾattâ āmartā) invites David to repudiate Adonijah's kingship on his own initiative rather than at Nathan's instruction.
1.25: kî yāraḏ hayyôm wayyizbaḥ šôr ûmĕrîʾ wĕṣōʾn lārōḇ: 'For today he has gone down and sacrificed oxen and fattened cattle and sheep in abundance.' The third repetition of the feast-detail (vv9, 19, 25) drives the point into David's consciousness. The phrase wayyōmal wayyēšt lĕp̄ānāyw wayyōmĕrû yĕḥî hammmelek ʾAḏōniyyāhû (and they are eating and drinking before him and saying, Long live King Adonijah!) mimics the exact formula that will be used for Solomon (v39: yĕḥî hammmelek šĕlōmōh) — the same formal proclamation formula, applied to the wrong king. Nathan is dramatizing the urgency.
1.26: wĕlî ʾanî-ʿaḇdĕkā wĕlĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûlĕḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ ûlišĕlōmōh ʿaḇdĕkā lōʾ qārāʾ: The list of those not invited corresponds exactly to the faction listed in v8 as loyal to Solomon. Nathan articulates his own exclusion alongside Zadok, Benaiah, and Solomon — placing himself in the company of the legitimate succession supporters. The self-description as ʿaḇdĕkā (your servant) throughout these verses is court protocol, not merely deference; it signals loyal covenant-servant status.
1.27: ʾim mēʾēṯ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek nihyâ haddāḇār hazzeh: 'Was this thing done by my lord the king?' The question is designed to force David's hand: either the succession was deliberately given to Adonijah (in which case Nathan and the others need to know) or it was not (in which case David must act). Nathan's closing wĕlōʾ hôḏaʿtā ʾeṯ-ʿaḇdĕkā mî yēšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʾaḥărāyw (and you have not informed your servant who would sit on the throne) is deniably accusatory — you left us in the dark — pressing for explicit royal declaration.
1.28: wayyaʿan hammmelek Dāwîḏ wayyōʾmer qirĕʾû-lî lĕḇaṯ-šeḇaʿ: 'King David answered and said, Call Bathsheba to me.' David's response is immediate — he calls Bathsheba back in (she had presumably left when Nathan arrived) and delivers his oath. The scene pivots: David moves from passivity (lying in the inner chamber unable to get warm) to decisive royal action. The oath formula he pronounces (v29-30) is the most formal possible: sworn by YHWH, invoking his own redemption from adversity.
1.29: ḥay YHWH ʾăšer pāḏâ ʾeṯ-nap̄šî mikkol-ṣārâ: 'As YHWH lives, who has redeemed my soul from all adversity.' The oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives) is the standard Hebrew oath-swearing formula, invoking YHWH as the living guarantor of the pledge. The relative clause — 'who has redeemed my soul from all adversity' — makes the oath deeply personal: it invokes David's entire history of divine deliverance (the theme of 2 Sam 22 = Psalm 18, where YHWH is the one who rescued David from all enemies). The verb pāḏâ (to redeem by paying a price, H6299) is the covenantal redemption term, distinct from gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer).
1.30: kî kaʾăšer nišbaʿtî lāḵ bĕYHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr kî-šĕlōmōh binkā yimlōk ʾaḥărāy: 'Just as I swore to you by YHWH the God of Israel saying, Solomon your son shall reign after me.' The oath is now confirmed — nišbaʿtî (I swore) + the specific content + the formula kî ḵēn ʾeʿĕśeh hayyôm hazzeh (so I will do this day). The immediate execution ('this day') is the characteristic urgency of royal decree in the Samuel-Kings narrative: when the king decides, action follows without delay. The three witnesses to this re-confirmation of the oath are Bathsheba, Nathan, and implicitly Abishag who is present.
1.31: waṯiqqōḏ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾappāyw ʾarṣâ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek waṯōʾmer yĕḥî ʾăḏōnî hammmelek Dāwîḏ lĕʿōlām: 'Bathsheba bowed with her face to the ground and prostrated herself to the king and said, May my lord King David live forever!' The proclamation yĕḥî hammmelek (long live the king!) + his name + lĕʿōlām (forever) is the formal royal acclamation. Bathsheba's response is the formal court expression of gratitude and loyalty. The lĕʿōlām (forever) is formulaic courtesy, not a literal claim of immortality — similar expressions appear throughout the ancient Near East as royal acclamation formulae.
1.32: wayyōʾmer hammmelek Dāwîḏ qirĕʾû-lî lĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûlĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ ûlĭḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ: David summons the three key actors for Solomon's anointing: Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the military commander. The triad represents the three institutions of legitimate authority: priestly, prophetic, and military. This is the institutional support structure that legitimizes the succession. Notably absent: Joab (the military commander who went with Adonijah) and Abiathar (the priest who supported Adonijah). The succession is constituted by the replacement faction.
1.33: wĕhirkabtem ʾeṯ-binkā šĕlōmōh ʿal-happirĕḏâ ʾăšer-lî: 'Have my son Solomon ride on my own mule.' The king's personal mule (pirĕḏâ) is the most important detail of the anointing procession: in the ancient Near East, riding the king's personal animal was equivalent to a public declaration of succession. David's mule is the physical symbol of royal authority being transferred. Compare Absalom's mule in 2 Sam 18:9 — Absalom was riding 'the king's mule' when he got caught in the oak tree, suggesting that claiming the royal mule was already part of the succession-claim protocol.
1.34: ûmāšaḥ ʾōṯô šām Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ lĕmelek ʿal-yiśrāʾēl: 'And let Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anoint him there as king over Israel.' The double anointing — priest and prophet together — is unprecedented in the biblical narrative; David was anointed by Samuel alone (1 Sam 16:13), and Saul was anointed by Samuel alone (1 Sam 10:1). The double anointing of Solomon by both priest and prophet underscores the institutional legitimacy of his succession: priestly sanction and prophetic confirmation simultaneously. The wĕqĕraʾtem qôl haššôp̄ār (blow the shofar) announces the proclamation publicly.
1.35: ûḇāʾtem ʾaḥărāyw wĕḇāʾ wĕyāšaḇ ʿal-kissĕʾî ûhûʾ yimlōk taḥtāy: 'Come after him, and he shall come and sit on my throne, for he shall be king in my place.' The formula ûhûʾ yimlōk taḥtāy (he shall reign in my place) — taḥtāy (H8478) literally 'under me' or 'in my stead' — is the formal succession declaration.
